title: Shear-driven mixing of segregated granular materials
abstract: As granular materials flow and settle, interactions among particles of different sizes or properties drive mixing and segregation, producing rich dynamics that reshape systems ranging from industrial hoppers to planetary surfaces. A hallmark of such polydisperse flows is shear-driven size segregation, whereby particles rearrange so that larger grains migrate above smaller ones. Despite substantial progress in modelling granular flow and segregation, key questions concerning the underlying mechanisms remain unresolved. In particular, the physics of granular mixing -- the natural counterpart of segregation -- has received far less attention. Here, we investigate the dynamics of initially segregated granular materials driven out of equilibrium by external shear. We ask: what controls the extent and rate of segregation and mixing in a sheared granular flow? Answering this question is essential for understanding how external forcing disrupts stable and unstable particle configurations and for optimising processes that require controlled mixing. Using theoretical analysis and numerical experiments, we develop and validate a scaling framework that quantifies the mixing dynamics. Our results provide new insight into the physics of granular flows and lay the foundation for improved prediction and design in both natural and industrial settings.
summary: This paper investigates how external shear drives the mixing and segregation of granular materials with different particle sizes. The authors provide a predictive scaling framework that relates the thickness of the resulting mixing layer and the time taken to reach equilibrium to the Péclet number and particle-size ratio. The study shows that the final state of the system is independent of whether the initial state was stable or unstable. These findings are important for optimizing industrial processes and understanding natural phenomena like planetary surface dynamics.
main_problem: Understanding the competition between shear-driven segregation and diffusive remixing in polydisperse granular flows to determine what controls the extent and rate of mixing.
main_result: The development of a scaling framework that quantifies mixing-layer thickness and timescales, showing that the final mixed layer thickness is independent of initial configuration and scales inversely with the Péclet number.
method: Numerical integration of a coupled nonlinear PDE system using the Method of Lines (MOL) in Python, combined with analytical derivation of segregation and mixing metrics.
model_or_system: A non-cohesive, bidisperse granular medium of rigid spheres with two different diameters undergoing external shear, modeled via a continuum-based convective-diffusive transport equation.
key_observables: Degree of segregation (S_phi), mixing-layer thickness (delta_m), mixing degree (M_phi), and characteristic mixing timescales (tau_m).
important_parameters: Particle-size ratio (Rd), Péclet number (Pe) representing the ratio of segregation to diffusion, and the dimensionless coupling parameter (E).
main_assumptions: Assumes uniform pressure, uses a specific mu(I)-rheology for layer height, and relies on a continuum description that may require further validation via DEM or experiments.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows real-world examples of segregation; Figure 2 compares concentration profiles for stable vs. unstable initial conditions; Figure 3 shows temporal evolution of segregation; Figure 4 illustrates the calculation of mixing-layer thickness and identifies critical thresholds.
paper_structure: Introduction to granular segregation/mixing, description of the convective-diffusive model and numerical methods, presentation of scaling laws for layer thickness and timescales, and discussion of limitations and future extensions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
